title: The Enlightenment and Origins of Revolution
description: This exercise will cover a discussion of the Enlightenment as an introduction to global revolutions. As such, this discussion will cover the Enlightenment through the themes of CDI and SIO. The items in this exercise will be grouped according to theme and will be scaled with diverse examples. Each of these themes will have a type and each type will scale 5 items, for a total of 12.
topic_path: The Enlightenment and Origins of Revolution

assessment_items.question_content:
**Use the excerpt below to answer the question that follows.**

>To understand political power right ... we must consider, what state all men are naturally in, and that is, a state of perfect freedom to order their actions, …A state also of equality ... no one having more than another; ...MEN being, as has been said, by nature, all free, equal, and independent, no one can be put out of this estate, and subjected to the political power of another, without his own consent…
>
>The only way whereby any one divests himself of his natural liberty, and puts on the bonds of civil society, is by agreeing with other men to join and unite into a community for their comfortable, safe, and peaceable living one amongst another, in a secure enjoyment of their properties, and a greater security against any, that are not of it. …

Source: John Locke, *The Second Treatise of Civil Government,* 1690, England. 

**Which of the following best summarizes the broad historical context for the excerpt above?** 

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: the Enlightenment
the Renaissance
the Reformation
the Industrial Revolution

assessment_items.question_content:
**Use the excerpt below to answer the question that follows.**

>To understand political power right ... we must consider, what state all men are naturally in, and that is, a state of perfect freedom to order their actions, …A state also of equality ... no one having more than another; ...MEN being, as has been said, by nature, all free, equal, and independent, no one can be put out of this estate, and subjected to the political power of another, without his own consent…
>
>The only way whereby any one divests himself of his natural liberty, and puts on the bonds of civil society, is by agreeing with other men to join and unite into a community for their comfortable, safe, and peaceable living one amongst another, in a secure enjoyment of their properties, and a greater security against any, that are not of it. …

Source: John Locke, *The Second Treatise of Civil Government,* 1690, England.

**Which of the following would best support the author's assertion regarding the role of the government from 1750 to 1900 CE?** 

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: It should be formed with a contract between the people and their ruler.
It should be based on the religious authority of the ruler.
It should ensure that all people are treated equally, no matter what.
It should be formed from the belief in the divine right of kings.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Use the excerpt below to answer the question that follows.**

>To understand political power right ... we must consider, what state all men are naturally in, and that is, a state of perfect freedom to order their actions, …A state also of equality ... no one having more than another; ...MEN being, as has been said, by nature, all free, equal, and independent, no one can be put out of this estate, and subjected to the political power of another, without his own consent…
>
>The only way whereby any one divests himself of his natural liberty, and puts on the bonds of civil society, is by agreeing with other men to join and unite into a community for their comfortable, safe, and peaceable living one amongst another, in a secure enjoyment of their properties, and a greater security against any, that are not of it. …

Source: John Locke, *The Second Treatise of Civil Government,* 1690, England. 

**Sentiments such as those expressed in the excerpt above contributed to which of the following types of rebellions from 1750 to 1900 CE?** 

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: revolutions against absolutist governments
movements to reform economic systems
rebellions to reform  the Catholic Church
protests opposing industrialization

assessment_items.question_content:
**Use the excerpt below to answer the question that follows.**

>Man was born free, but everywhere he is in chains. This man believes that he is the master of others, and still he is more of a slave than they are...The social contract’s terms, when they are well understood, can be reduced to a single stipulation: the individual member alienates himself totally to the whole community together with all his rights...In reality, each individual may have one particular will as a man that is different from-or contrary to-the general will which he has as a citizen…
>
>Whatever benefits he had in the state of nature but lost in the civil state, a man gains more than enough new ones to make up for them. His capabilities are put to good use and developed; his ideas are enriched, his sentiments made more noble, and his soul elevated. ...The first and most important conclusion from the principles we have established thus far is that the general will alone may direct the forces of the State to achieve the goal for which it was founded, the common good. ... 

Source: Jean-Jacques Rousseau, *The Social Contract,* 1762, France. 

**During the Enlightenment, attitudes such as the one expressed in the excerpt contributed most directly to which of the following?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: the emphasis on individual reason and the social contract over religious ideals in government
the emphasis on political power being derived from divine power
the emphasis on total equality in the public sphere and the social contract
the emphasis on the natural order of man and the reinforcement of social hierarchies like serfdom

assessment_items.question_content:
**Use the excerpt below to answer the question that follows.**

>Man was born free, but everywhere he is in chains. This man believes that he is the master of others, and still he is more of a slave than they are...The social contract’s terms, when they are well understood, can be reduced to a single stipulation: the individual member alienates himself totally to the whole community together with all his rights...In reality, each individual may have one particular will as a man that is different from-or contrary to-the general will which he has as a citizen…
>
>Whatever benefits he had in the state of nature but lost in the civil state, a man gains more than enough new ones to make up for them. His capabilities are put to good use and developed; his ideas are enriched, his sentiments made more noble, and his soul elevated. ...The first and most important conclusion from the principles we have established thus far is that the general will alone may direct the forces of the State to achieve the goal for which it was founded, the common good. ... 

Source: Jean-Jacques Rousseau, *The Social Contract*, 1762, France. OER

**The sentiments expressed in the excerpt above best illustrate which of the following aspects of the Enlightenment?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: the concept of sovereignty
the concept of equality
the emphasis on free markets
the emphasis on colonial authority

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following impacts of the Enlightenment helped to fuel many rebellions against colonial governments from 1750 to 1900 CE?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: the rise of nationalism
free market taxation policies
the rise of power of the Church
the concept of property rights

assessment_items.question_content:
**Use the excerpt below to answer the question that follows.**

>We were not many days in the merchant's custody before we were sold after their usual manner, which is this:—On a signal given, (as the beat of a drum) the buyers rush at once into the yard where the slaves are confined, and make choice of that parcel they like best. … In this manner, without scruple, are relations and friends separated, most of them never to see each other again. 
>
>I remember in the vessel in which I was brought over, in the men's apartment, there were several brothers, who, in the sale, were sold in different lots; and it was very moving on this occasion to see and hear their cries at parting. O, ye nominal Christians! might not an African ask you, learned you this from your God, who says unto you, Do unto all men as you would men should do unto you?

Source: Olaudah Equiano, *The Interesting Narrative of the Life of Olaudah Equiano*, 1789. Public domain. 

**The sentiments expressed in the excerpt above best illustrate which of the following aspects of the Enlightenment?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Reform movements brought on by Enlightenment thinking contributed to the abolition of slavery.
Enlightenment ideals such as property rights reinforced the institution of slavery.
The rise of Christian thought led to the moral outrage and the abolition of slavery.
Enlightenment ideals such as the social contract inspired decolonization movements in Africa.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Use the excerpt below to answer the question that follows.**

>We were not many days in the merchant's custody before we were sold after their usual manner, which is this:—On a signal given, (as the beat of a drum) the buyers rush at once into the yard where the slaves are confined, and make choice of that parcel they like best. … In this manner, without scruple, are relations and friends separated, most of them never to see each other again. 
>
>I remember in the vessel in which I was brought over, in the men's apartment, there were several brothers, who, in the sale, were sold in different lots; and it was very moving on this occasion to see and hear their cries at parting. O, ye nominal Christians! might not an African ask you, learned you this from your God, who says unto you, Do unto all men as you would men should do unto you?

Source: Olaudah Equiano, *The Interesting Narrative of the Life of Olaudah Equiano*, 1789. Public domain. 

**Which of the following theories from 1750 to 1900 CE about why slavery was abolished does the above excerpt best support?** 

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Actions taken by Black people led to the end of slavery.
People who work should be paid for their labor.
Slavery was unfair competition for other businesses.
Governments should derive their power from the consent of the people.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Use the excerpt below to answer the question that follows.**

>We were not many days in the merchant's custody before we were sold after their usual manner, which is this:—On a signal given, (as the beat of a drum) the buyers rush at once into the yard where the slaves are confined, and make choice of that parcel they like best. … In this manner, without scruple, are relations and friends separated, most of them never to see each other again. 
>
>I remember in the vessel in which I was brought over, in the men's apartment, there were several brothers, who, in the sale, were sold in different lots; and it was very moving on this occasion to see and hear their cries at parting. O, ye nominal Christians! might not an African ask you, learned you this from your God, who says unto you, Do unto all men as you would men should do unto you?

Source: Olaudah Equiano, *The Interesting Narrative of the Life of Olaudah Equiano*, 1789. Public domain. 

**The author of the excerpt above most likely wrote this narrative in order to achieve which of the following from  1750 to 1900 CE?** 

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: the end of slavery
the right of popular sovereignty
the beginning of a social contract
the end of colonization

assessment_items.question_content:
**Use the excerpt below to answer the question that follows.**

>My own sex, I hope, will excuse me, if I treat them like rational creatures, instead of flattering their fascinating graces, and viewing them as if they were in a state of perpetual childhood, unable to stand alone. 
>
>I earnestly wish to point out in what true dignity and human happiness consists–I wish to persuade women to endeavour to acquire strength, both of mind and body, and to convince them that the soft phrases, susceptibility of heart, delicacy of sentiment, and refinement of taste, are almost synonymous with epithets of weakness, and that those beings who are only the objects of pity and that kind of love, which has been termed its sister, will soon become objects of contempt.


Source: Mary Wollstonecroft, *A Vindication of the Rights of Woman,* 1792. Public domain. 

**Which of the following best exemplifies the broad historical context in which the above excerpt was written?** 

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Enlightenment thought inspired the women’s suffrage movement.
The Industrial Revolution led to an increase in economic opportunities for women.
The American Revolution advocated for an increase in women’s rights.
Reformation ideals led to many women advocating for increased standing in the church.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Use the excerpt below to answer the question that follows.**

>My own sex, I hope, will excuse me, if I treat them like rational creatures, instead of flattering their fascinating graces, and viewing them as if they were in a state of perpetual childhood, unable to stand alone. 
>
>I earnestly wish to point out in what true dignity and human happiness consists–I wish to persuade women to endeavour to acquire strength, both of mind and body, and to convince them that the soft phrases, susceptibility of heart, delicacy of sentiment, and refinement of taste, are almost synonymous with epithets of weakness, and that those beings who are only the objects of pity and that kind of love, which has been termed its sister, will soon become objects of contempt.

Source: Mary Wollstonecroft, *A Vindication of the Rights of Woman*, 1792. Public domain. 

**Which of the following best explains the impact of the Enlightenment on societies in the time period from 1750–1900?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: It challenged existing government structures and inspired women’s suffrage movements that challenged existing gender hierarchies.
It led to a rise in atheism and energized many movements for social reform.
It encouraged women to gain employment outside the home in order to advocate for equality.
It influenced women to preserve traditional roles by reinforcing existing gender hierarchies in a time of widespread political upheaval.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following could a historian use to support the claim that the Enlightenment only brought about limited progress?** 

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Women in most areas of the world did not receive the right to vote until well into the 19th century.
There were no examples of the practical application of Enlightenment ideals.
The slave trade intensified in Europe and the Americas.
The Catholic Church maintained its absolute power in most areas of the world.
